perturbation.original_text: Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of ten (10) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.explanation: The modification extends the confidentiality period to ten years, which may be considered unreasonably long and thus unenforceable under New York law, depending on the nature of the information and the industry.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York courts may find a 10-year confidentiality term unreasonable and unenforceable depending on the specific facts and circumstances.
perturbation.law_citation: Reed, Roberts Assocs., Inc. v. Strauman, 40 N.Y.2d 303, 307, 353 N.E.2d 590, 593, 386 N.Y.S.2d 677, 679 (1976)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires that non-compete and confidentiality agreements be reasonable in time and scope. A ten-year confidentiality period may be seen as excessive, particularly if the information is likely to become obsolete or public during that time, or if the restriction imposes undue hardship on the Seller. Reasonableness is based on industry standards, the nature of the confidential information, and the impact on the restricted party's ability to earn a living.
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the confidentiality period from three to ten years. The Law Explanation states that New York law requires non-compete and confidentiality agreements to be reasonable in time and scope. The Law Explanation further suggests a ten-year period may be seen as excessive. Although there are no scraped law snippets to confirm this, the Perturbation Explanation supports the Law Explanation, stating the modification may be considered unreasonably long, which creates a legal contradiction based on the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition that the contract may be violating state law.

perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that for a period of ten (10) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to ten years makes it potentially unreasonable and unenforceable under New York law, which requires non-compete agreements to be limited in time and scope to protect legitimate business interests.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York courts scrutinize the reasonableness of non-compete agreements, and a ten-year term may be deemed excessive, especially given the dynamic nature of the health care products market.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law assesses non-compete agreements based on their reasonableness, considering factors like duration, geographic scope, and hardship to the employee. A ten-year restriction on competing in the health care products market is likely to be viewed as overly broad and unduly burdensome, making it unenforceable unless the employer can demonstrate an exceptionally strong justification.

Hirshberg Annotate this Case 93 N.Y.2d 382 (1999) 712 N.E.2d 1220 690 N.Y.S.2d 854 BDO SEIDMAN, Appellant, v. JEFFREY HIRSHBERG, Respondent. Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued March 24, 1999. Decided May 13, 1999. *385 Willkie Farr & Gallagher, New York City (Michael R. Young and Jeffrey O. Grossman of counsel), Kavinoky & Cook, L. L. P., Buffalo (Joseph J. Welter of counsel), Scott M. Univer and Barbara A. Taylor, of the Illinois Bar, admitted pro hac vice, for appellant. Jaeckle Fleischmann & Mugel, L. L. P., Buffalo (Philip H. McIntyre and Jennifer M. Demert of counsel), for respondent. *386 Vedder, Price, Kaufman, Kammholz & Day, New York City (Dan L. Goldwasser and Michael J. Crisafulli of counsel), for New York State Society of Certified Public Accountants, amicus curiae. Chief Judge KAYE and Judges BELLACOSA, SMITH, CIPARICK, WESLEY and ROSENBLATT concur. *387 OPINION OF THE COURT LEVINE, J. BDO Seidman (BDO), a general partnership of certified public accountants, appeals from the affirmance of an order of Supreme Court granting summary judgment dismissing its complaint against defendant, who was formerly employed as an accountant with the firm. The central issue before us is whether the "reimbursement clause" in an agreement between the parties, requiring defendant to compensate BDO for serving any client of the firm's Buffalo office within 18 months after the termination of his employment, is an invalid and unenforceable restrictive covenant. The courts below so held. Facts and Procedural History BDO is a national accounting firm having 40 offices throughout the United States, including four in New York State. Defendant began employment in BDO's Buffalo office in 1984, when the accounting firm he had been working for was merged into BDO, its partners becoming BDO partners. In 1989, defendant was promoted to the position of manager, apparently a step immediately below attaining partner status. As a condition of receiving the promotion, defendant was required to sign a "Manager's Agreement," the provisions o
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from three to ten years. The Perturbation Explanation states that this extension may be unreasonable and unenforceable under New York law, because it is not limited in time and scope. The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating that New York law assesses non-compete agreements based on their reasonableness, including duration. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses a case (BDO Seidman v. Hirshberg) regarding a restrictive covenant, indicating that New York courts do scrutinize such agreements. While Snippet 1 doesn't specify a time limit, the explanations provided state that a ten-year restriction is likely overly broad, suggesting a violation of the principle of reasonableness as applied by New York courts.

perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that for a period of five (5) years after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be seen as unreasonably long under New York law, potentially restricting the seller's ability to engage in legitimate business activities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York courts evaluate non-solicitation clauses for reasonableness, and a five-year term may be viewed as excessive depending on the industry and the specific circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires that non-solicitation agreements be reasonably limited in time and scope to protect the employer's legitimate interests without unduly restricting the former employee's ability to earn a living or conduct business. A five-year restriction on soliciting employees is likely to be challenged as overly broad, especially if the former employee's new role does not directly compete or if the targeted employees are not in key positions.
perturbation.justification: The original contract restricts the seller from soliciting employees for one year, while the changed text extends this period to five years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a five-year non-solicitation period may be deemed unreasonably long under New York law, potentially hindering the seller's ability to engage in legitimate business activities. The lack of scraped law snippets does not invalidate the claim that extending the non-solicitation period creates a legal contradiction because the generated explanations outline how this change likely violates established legal principles regarding reasonable limitations on non-solicitation agreements. Therefore, the change introduces a time-sensitive obligation that may violate state law, leading to an unenforceable term.

perturbation.original_text: Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are not an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to an injunction, without the necessity of posting any bond, enjoining, prohibiting, and restraining Seller from the continuance of any such violation, in addition to any monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are not an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to an injunction, with the necessity of posting a nominal bond of $100, enjoining, prohibiting, and restraining Seller from the continuance of any such violation, in addition to any monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying a nominal bond amount may not comply with New York's requirements for injunctions. A bond should be sufficient to cover potential damages to the enjoined party if the injunction is later found to be wrongly issued.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law requires that a bond posted for a preliminary injunction be sufficient to cover the damages incurred by the enjoined party if the injunction is later determined to have been improperly granted.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR 6312(b) mandates that a party seeking a preliminary injunction provide an undertaking (bond) to cover potential damages if the injunction is later deemed unwarranted. Setting a nominal bond of $100 contradicts the purpose of the undertaking, as it is unlikely to adequately compensate the enjoined party for losses sustained during the injunction period. Courts have the discretion to determine the appropriate bond amount based on the potential harm to the defendant.

Motion papers; undertaking; issues of fact. (a) Affidavit; other evidence. On a motion for a preliminary injunction the plaintiff shall show, by affidavit and such other evidence as may be submitted, that there is a cause of action, and either that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action and tending to render the judgment ineffectual; or that the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. (b) Undertaking. Except as provided in section 2512 and in actions brought under section two hundred sixty-five-a of the real property law, prior to the granting of a preliminary injunction, the plaintiff shall give an undertaking in an amount to be fixed by the court, that the plaintiff, if it is finally determined that he or she was not entitled to an injunction, will pay to the defendant all damages and costs which may be sustained by reason of the injunction, including: 1. if the injunction is to stay proceedings in another action, on any ground other than that a report, verdict or decision was obtained by actual fraud, all damages and costs which may be, or which have been, awarded in the other action to the defendant as well as all damages and costs which may be awarded him or her in the action in which the injunction was granted; or, 2. if the injunction is to stay proceedings in an action to recover real property, or for dower, on any ground other than that a verdict, report or decision was obtained by actual fraud, all damages and costs which may be, or which have been, awarded to the defendant in the action in which the injunction was gran
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for an injunction without posting a bond. The changed text specifies a nominal bond of $100. According to the Law Explanation, this may contradict the purpose of an undertaking, as it may not adequately compensate the enjoined party for losses. The Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CPLR 6312(b), states the plaintiff 'shall give an undertaking in an amount to be fixed by the court, that the plaintiff... will pay to the defendant all damages and costs which may be sustained by reason of the injunction'. A nominal bond of $100 is unlikely to cover actual damages, thus contradicting the requirement for an adequate undertaking and creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The terms of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California without considering conflict of laws principles may violate New York's established choice-of-law rules if the agreement has significant connections to New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York courts apply a 'grouping of contacts' or 'center of gravity' approach to choice-of-law analysis, and changing the governing law without considering this may be invalid.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts do not automatically apply the law specified in a contract's choice-of-law provision. Instead, they utilize a 'grouping of contacts' approach to determine the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship to the transaction. Changing the governing law to California without considering the agreement's connections to New York (e.g., where the contract was negotiated, signed, or performed) may contradict New York's choice-of-law rules and render the California law provision unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Matter of Allstate Ins. Co.(stolarz-Njm) :: 1993 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Matter of Allstate Ins. Co.(stolarz-Njm) Annotate this Case 81 N.Y.2d 219 (1993) In the Matter of the Arbitration between Allstate Insurance Company, Petitioner, and Kathleen Stolarz et al., Respondents; New Jersey Manufacturers Insurance Company, Appellant. Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued February 11, 1993. Decided May 4, 1993. Downing & Mehrtens, P. C., New York City (Thomas E. Mehrtens, Alison D. Metzler and John M. Downing of counsel), for appellant. Michael R. Scolnick, P. C., Pomona (Michael R. Scolnick and Valerie Crown-Goldstein of counsel), for respondents. Judges SIMONS, TITONE, BELLACOSA and SMITH concur with Chief Judge KAYE; Judge HANCOCK, JR., dissents and votes to affirm in a separate opinion. *222Chief Judge KAYE. In this automobile insurance dispute, both Supreme Court and the Appellate Division concluded that there was a conflict between New York and New Jersey law, and that New York law should control. We conclude there is no such conflict, and in any event New Jersey law applies. Accordingly, we reverse. I. On February 18, 1989, Kathleen Stolarz and her husband were injured in a two-car accident on Route 6 in Woodbury, New York. The Stolarz vehicle was a company car leased by her employer, Blue Cross/Blue Shield of New Jersey, and registered in New Jersey. New Jersey Manufacturers Insurance Company (NJM) insured the vehicle under a policy issued to Blue Cross and written to conform to New Jersey law. Stolarz regularly garaged the vehicle at her home in Monroe, New York, a few miles north of the New Jersey border. The carrier insuring the other vehicle paid the Stolarzes $20,000, the liability limits of its insured's policy. When Allstate Insurance Company, the insurer of the Stolarzes' personal cars, disputed the amount payable under that policy's underinsurance coverage, the Stolarzes served a demand for arbitration and Allstate,
perturbation.justification: The change from New York to California law, without regard to conflict of laws principles, directly contradicts established legal procedure in New York. As the LLM explanation states, New York courts use a 'grouping of contacts' approach rather than automatically applying the chosen law. The scraped snippet, Matter of Allstate Ins. Co.(stolarz-Njm), while not directly stating this principle, shows that choice of law is not automatic and is subject to judicial review based on the specifics of the case. Therefore, by changing the governing law and disregarding conflict of laws, the modified text creates a situation where the contract might not be enforced under New York law, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' type.
